stage.name: Trading Fundamentals (High School)
stage.description: Candlestick charts, support and resistance, RSI and MACD plain-language, order types, risk/reward, and trading psychology — intermediate level.

elements.content: The language every trader needs to read price action — High School Level
Each candle shows: Open, High, Low, Close. The body = range between open and close. Wicks = highest and lowest price touched. Green = closed higher. Red = closed lower.

Bullish Engulfing

Large green candle engulfs prior red. Strong reversal at downtrend bottom.

Doji

Open ≈ Close. Buyers and sellers equally matched. Indecision — wait for confirmation.

Shooting Star

Long upper wick, small body. Rejected after rally. Bearish reversal signal.

Support: Price zone where buying has historically stopped declines. Tested and held multiple times = stronger support. Buy near support, stop-loss just below.

Resistance: Price zone where selling has historically capped rallies. Breaking through is bullish. Look to reduce near resistance, watch for breakout.

Role Reversal: Old resistance becomes new support after a breakout — one of the most reliable TA patterns.

actions.text: Candlestick Charts and Support and Resistance — High School Level

Each candlestick shows four data points: Open, High, Low, and Close. The body is the range between open and close. Wicks show the highest and lowest prices touched during the period. Green candles closed higher — buyers won. Red candles closed lower — sellers won.
Bullish Engulfing: a large green candle completely engulfs the previous red candle. Buyers overwhelmed sellers. A strong reversal signal at the bottom of a downtrend.

elements.content: Recognizing patterns and applying them in real markets
⚡ Key Rule: No single candle gives a complete picture. Context matters — where is this candle forming? At support? After a trend? Candlesticks are clues, not certainties. Always confirm with volume.

actions.text: Doji: open and close are almost identical — tiny body, long wicks. Buyers and sellers are equally matched. The market is undecided. Usually precedes a big move in either direction — wait for confirmation.
Shooting Star: long upper wick, small body at the bottom. Price rallied hard but was rejected — sellers pushed it back down. A bearish reversal signal after an uptrend.

Support is a price zone where buying has historically stopped declines. The more times it's tested and held, the stronger it is. Resistance is a zone where selling has historically capped rallies. Breaking through resistance convincingly is a bullish signal. And role reversal: old resistance becomes new support after a breakout — one of the most reliable patterns in technical analysis.

Key rule: No single candle gives a complete picture. Context matters — where is this candle forming? At support? After a trend? Candlesticks are clues, not certainties. Always confirm with volume and other signals.

elements.content: What these tools measure and why the mental game is harder than the technical game

Measures overbought/oversold on 0–100 scale. Default 14-period lookback.
RSI above 70 → Overbought, potential pullback
RSI below 30 → Oversold, potential bounce
RSI at 50 → Neutral, trend undecided
Caveat: in strong trends, RSI stays extreme. Don't short just because RSI is 70.

Tracks difference between 12-period and 26-period EMAs. Signal line = 9-period EMA of MACD.
Bullish cross: MACD crosses above signal → buy signal
Bearish cross: MACD crosses below signal → sell signal
Divergence: Price new high but MACD doesn't → weakening momentum

actions.text: RSI, MACD, Order Types, and Trading Psychology

RSI measures overbought and oversold conditions on a zero to one hundred scale using a 14-period lookback. RSI above 70 signals overbought — potential pullback ahead. RSI below 30 signals oversold — potential bounce. RSI at 50 is neutral. Important caveat: in strong trends, RSI can stay extreme for extended periods. Don't short just because RSI hits 70.

scenes.title: RSI, MACD, Order Types & Psychology (Part B)

elements.content: How to place orders and manage the emotional side of trading

Market: Execute now at best price. Guaranteed fill, accept spread.
Limit: Only fill at your price or better. Not guaranteed.
Stop-Loss: Auto-exit if price falls to your set level. Defines max loss.
Take-Profit: Auto-exit at target. Lock in gains without watching.
R:R Ratio: Risk $100 to make $200 = 1:2. Target min 1:2.

FOMO — Chasing coins after they're already up 50%
Loss Aversion — Holding losers hoping they come back
Overtrading — Forced entries with negative expected value
Revenge Trading — Doubling down after a loss to recover

actions.text: MACD tracks the difference between the 12-period and 26-period exponential moving averages. A signal line is the 9-period EMA of the MACD itself. Bullish cross: MACD crosses above the signal line — buy signal. Bearish cross: MACD crosses below — sell signal. Divergence: price makes a new high but MACD doesn't — momentum is weakening, watch out.

Order types: Market orders execute immediately at the best available price — guaranteed fill. Limit orders only fill at your specified price or better — not guaranteed. Stop-loss orders auto-exit your position if price falls to your set level — this defines your maximum loss before you enter. Take-profit orders lock in gains automatically at your target. And always know your risk-reward ratio — risk one hundred dollars to make two hundred gives you a 1 to 2 ratio. Professionals target minimum 1 to 2.

Studies show 70 to 80 percent of retail traders lose money — not from lack of indicators, but from psychology. FOMO: chasing coins after they're already up fifty percent. Loss aversion: holding losers hoping they come back, turning small losses into account-wrecking drawdowns. Overtrading: forced entries with no edge. Revenge trading: doubling down after a loss to recover — this is how accounts blow up. Copy trading bypasses all of this by following disciplined traders automatically.

elements.content: The two most important things to confirm before any trade

Price moves in trends — uptrend (higher highs, higher lows), downtrend (lower highs, lower lows), or sideways. Trading with the trend is far safer than against it.


Volume confirms trend strength. A price breakout on high volume is reliable. The same breakout on low volume is likely a fake-out. Always confirm moves with volume before entering a position.

actions.text: 📊 Trends & Volume
The two most important things to confirm before any trade

Price moves in trends — uptrend (higher highs, higher lows), downtrend (lower highs, lower lows), or sideways. Trading with the trend is far safer than against it. Volume confirms trend strength. A price breakout on high volume is reliable. The same breakout on low volume is likely a fake-out. Always confirm moves with volume before entering a position.
⬆️ Uptrend. Higher highs, higher lows
⬇️ Downtrend. Lower highs, lower lows
📊 Volume. Confirms or denies the move
⚠️ Low Volume Breakout. Often reverses quickly

elements.content: The three indicators every trader should understand

RSI (Relative Strength Index) measures momentum 0–100. Above 70 = overbought (potential sell). Below 30 = oversold (potential buy). A useful early warning system, not a standalone signal.


MACD shows momentum shifts. When the MACD line crosses above the signal line = bullish. Below = bearish.


Moving Averages (MA) smooth out price noise. The 50-day and 200-day MAs are widely watched. When 50 crosses above 200 = Golden Cross (very bullish). Opposite = Death Cross.

actions.text: 📉 RSI, MACD & Moving Averages
The three indicators every trader should understand

RSI (Relative Strength Index) measures momentum 0–100. Above 70 = overbought (potential sell). Below 30 = oversold (potential buy). A useful early warning system, not a standalone signal. MACD shows momentum shifts. When the MACD line crosses above the signal line = bullish. Below = bearish. Moving Averages (MA) smooth out price noise. The 50-day and 200-day MAs are widely watched. When 50 crosses above 200 = Golden Cross (very bullish). Opposite = Death Cross.
📊 RSI. Momentum — 30 buy / 70 sell
⚡ MACD. Momentum crossover signal
〰️ Moving Avg. Trend direction + key levels
✨ Golden Cross. 50MA crosses above 200MA

elements.content: The math that separates profitable traders from losers

Every trade has a risk-reward ratio. If you risk $100 to potentially make $300, your ratio is 1:3. Professional traders require at least 1:2 before entering any trade.


With a 1:2 ratio, you can be right only 40% of the time and still be profitable. Math beats emotion. Set your stop-loss and take-profit levels before entering — never move your stop-loss to avoid a loss. That is how small losses become account-destroying losses.

🛑 Stop Loss
Set before entry — never move it

🎯 Take Profit
Know your exit before you enter

actions.text: ⚖️ Risk-Reward Ratio
The math that separates profitable traders from losers

Every trade has a risk-reward ratio . If you risk $100 to potentially make $300, your ratio is 1:3. Professional traders require at least 1:2 before entering any trade. With a 1:2 ratio, you can be right only 40% of the time and still be profitable. Math beats emotion. Set your stop-loss and take-profit levels before entering — never move your stop-loss to avoid a loss. That is how small losses become account-destroying losses.
📐 1:2 R:R. Risk $1 to make $2
🛑 Stop Loss. Set before entry — never move it
🎯 Take Profit. Know your exit before you enter
🧮 Math. 40% win rate + 1:2 = profitable

questions.question: What does an RSI reading above 70 indicate?

options.label: The asset is a strong buy
The asset is overbought — a pullback may be coming
The trading volume is very high
A bullish MACD crossover has occurred

questions.analysis: RSI above 70 means the asset has gained significantly in a short period and may be due for a pullback — it's 'overbought' relative to recent price history. However, in strong uptrends, RSI can stay above 70 for extended periods. Always wait for RSI to turn before acting on it.

questions.question: What is 'role reversal' in support and resistance?

options.label: When buyers and sellers switch roles
When a resistance level, once broken, becomes the new support
When a chart pattern reverses direction
When RSI moves from overbought to oversold

questions.analysis: Role reversal: when price breaks convincingly above a resistance level, that level often becomes the new support on a pullback. Old resistance becomes new support (and vice versa). Traders use retests of these flipped levels for high-probability entries.

questions.question: What is the difference between a limit order and a market order?

options.label: Market orders are only available on CEXs; limit orders only on DEXs
A market order executes immediately at current price; a limit order only fills at your specified price or better
Limit orders always fill faster than market orders
Market orders require more collateral

questions.analysis: A market order executes immediately at whatever the best available price is — guaranteed to fill. A limit order specifies the maximum price you'll pay (or minimum you'll sell for) — it only fills if the market reaches that level, giving you price control but no fill guarantee.

questions.question: What is 'FOMO' in trading?

options.label: A type of chart pattern
Fear of Missing Out — buying into a move after it's already happened, often near the top
A method for calculating position size
A strategy for timing market entries

questions.analysis: FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) is the emotional impulse to buy a coin after it's already rallied sharply. You buy because you're afraid of missing further gains — but you often end up buying the top while smart money is already taking profits. FOMO entries are one of the most common causes of retail losses.

questions.question: If you risk $100 on a trade to potentially make $300, what is your risk/reward ratio?
questions.analysis: Risk/reward ratio is risk:reward. If you risk $100 to potentially gain $300, that's a 1:3 ratio — for every dollar risked, you aim to gain three. Professionals generally target a minimum of 1:2. With a 1:3 ratio and even a 40% win rate, you're profitable long-term.

questions.question: What does 'going long' mean?

options.label: Holding a position for more than one year
Betting the price will fall
Buying an asset expecting the price to rise
Trading on leverage only

questions.analysis: Going long means you buy an asset hoping to sell it later at a higher price. It's the most natural form of trading — buy low, sell high. The opposite (short selling) profits when price falls.

questions.question: If your risk-reward ratio is 1:3 and you risk $200, what is your target profit?
questions.analysis: A 1:3 risk-reward ratio means for every $1 risked, you target $3 in profit. Risking $200 × 3 = $600 target profit. With a 1:3 ratio, you only need to win 25% of trades to break even.

actions.text: Five questions on trading fundamentals — RSI, support and resistance, order types, and the psychology traps that trip up most retail traders.
Understanding copy trading at OnlyCrypto means understanding what makes a trader worth following. These concepts help you evaluate traders more clearly.
